title: norm
summary: Compute vector and matrix norms in MATLAB and RunMat.

gpu_support.notes: RunMat gathers to the host when specialized provider hooks are unavailable; providers may add custom norm kernels in the future.

description: `norm(X)` returns vector or matrix magnitudes. Default norms, alternate `p`/keyword options (including Frobenius and nuclear), and domain restrictions follow MATLAB semantics.

examples.description: Computing the Euclidean norm of a vector
Calculating the Frobenius norm of a matrix
Using the infinity norm for robust bounds
Summing singular values with the nuclear norm
Computing the norm of complex-valued data

examples.input:
G = gpuArray([3 4 12]);
mag = norm(G);      % falls back to CPU today; future providers can stay on device

faqs.question: What is the difference between `norm(x)` and `norm(x, 2)`?
faqs.answer: No difference. Both compute the Euclidean norm for vectors (or the spectral norm for matrices).

faqs.question: Does `norm` support fractional powers?
faqs.answer: Yes. For vectors you can pass any non-zero real scalar `p`, including fractional values. MATLAB’s special cases (`p = 0`, `Inf`, `-Inf`) are also supported.

faqs.question: Can I use `norm` on complex matrices?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. RunMat mirrors MATLAB by computing singular values in complex arithmetic and always returning a non-negative real scalar.

faqs.question: When should I use `'fro'` versus `'nuc'`?
faqs.answer: `'fro'` returns the square root of the sum of squares (robust and inexpensive). `'nuc'` sums singular values and is particularly useful in low-rank optimization problems.

faqs.question: Why does `norm` of an empty matrix return `0`?
faqs.answer: That matches MATLAB’s convention: the sum over an empty set is zero, so every supported norm returns `0` for empty inputs.

faqs.question: Can the norm overflow or underflow?
faqs.answer: Yes. The result obeys IEEE-754 double precision rules, just like MATLAB. Extremely large or tiny values may overflow to `Inf` or underflow toward zero.

faqs.question: Will the result stay on the GPU?
faqs.answer: The current in-process provider gathers the tensor to host memory. Providers that implement custom norm kernels can keep the computation entirely on device without user-visible changes.

gpu_residency:
```matlab:runnable
G = gpuArray([3 4 12]);
mag = norm(G);      % falls back to CPU today; future providers can stay on device
```
Expected output:
```matlab
mag = 13
```
gpu_behavior: When an acceleration provider is active, RunMat keeps GPU inputs resident. If the provider exposes a dedicated norm hook in the future it can execute entirely on the device. Until then RunMat gathers the input tensor to the host, computes the norm with the shared CPU implementation, and returns the scalar result. This mirrors MATLAB semantics while guaranteeing correctness even in the absence of specialized kernels.
